FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study baseline (2016-2018) and 1-year follow-up (2017-2019), N=8267 adolescents aged 9-10 years from 21 U.S. sites.

FT_IC2.evidence: Specific smartphone/social media variables: television, video games, videos (YouTube), texting, video chatting, social media. Youth Screen Time Survey measured hours per day separately for each modality.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regression analyses examining associations between screen time measures and MIND diet nutrition score (non-SM outcome). Both prospective and cross-sectional analyses with 16 individual regression models reported.

FT_IC4.evidence: β coefficients with 95% CIs reported in Table 2. Total screen time: β=-0.07 (95% CI: -0.09, -0.05) at year 0 and year 1; Texting: β=-0.15 (95% CI: -0.24, -0.05); Social media: β=-0.16 (95% CI: -0.28, -0.05) cross-sectionally.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD data; prospective and cross-sectional multiple linear regression models testing screen time associations with diet quality outcome.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Academic Pediatrics, 2024; 24:748-754.

FT_EC7_reason: Paper includes cross-sectional analyses (cross-sectional ignores direction per FT-EC7 rule). Additionally, outcome (nutrition/diet quality) is a psychological/behavioral variable, not a demographic predictor, so FT-EC7 does not apply.
notes: All screen time modalities measured separately and have individual coefficients extractable. Study meets all inclusion criteria.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study Year 3 (2019-2021), N=9859 adolescents, mostly 12-13 years old, from 21 U.S. recruitment sites.

FT_IC2.evidence: Specific smartphone/social media variables measured: recreational screen time by modality (TV/movies, single-player gaming, multi-player gaming, texting, social media, video chatting, internet browsing) and problematic use scales (SMAQ for social media, VGAQ for video games, MPIQ for mobile phones).

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regression analyses examining associations between gender identity (transgender/gender-questioning) and screen time modalities and problematic use measures. Tables 2 and 3 show regression coefficients with separate models for transgender and gender-questioning comparisons.

FT_IC4.evidence: β coefficients with 95% CIs reported in Tables 2 and 3. Examples: Transgender total screen time β=4.51 (95% CI 1.17-7.85); Video Game Addiction Questionnaire β=0.46 (95% CI 0.19-0.74); Social Media Addiction β=0.43 (95% CI 0.17-0.69).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis of ABCD Study Year 3 data with multiple linear regression models testing associations between gender identity and screen use outcomes.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Annals of Epidemiology, 2024; 95:6-11.

FT_EC7_reason: Phone/SM are not only dependent variables; gender identity is both a demographic characteristic AND an individual characteristic that reflects psychological/developmental differences in gender minority youth. Paper reports phone/SM as outcomes of gender identity, which includes psychological/clinical/behavioral dimensions. FT-EC7 does not apply when individual characteristics are involved.
notes: Problematic use scales (SMAQ, VGAQ, MPIQ) qualify as specific phone/SM variables per criteria. Both recreational and problematic use modalities separately measured.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study Years 2-3 (2018-2021), N=9,398 early adolescents aged 11-12 years from 21 U.S. sites.

FT_IC2.evidence: Specific bedtime screen use variables: watching movies/videos/TV, video games, music, talking on phone/texting, social media, chat rooms, internet browsing, computer/laptop for studying. Nine-item measure adapted from prior studies assessing screen use while in bed before sleep.

FT_IC3.evidence: Logistic regression models for total sleep disturbance; ordinal logistic regression for disorders of initiating/maintaining sleep; multiple linear regression for weekly sleep duration. Bedtime screen use (Year 2) as predictor of sleep outcomes (Year 3) with adjustments for baseline sleep variables.

FT_IC4.evidence: Odds ratios and β coefficients with 95% CIs reported in Table 3. Examples: TV/electronic device in bedroom β=-0.21 (95% CI -0.28, -0.13); Talking/texting phone OR=1.13 (95% CI 1.05-1.23); Social media OR=1.10 (95% CI 1.01-1.20); p-values reported throughout.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort analysis of ABCD data (Years 2-3) with regression models testing prospective associations between bedtime screen use and sleep outcomes one year later.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Journal of Adolescent Health, 2024; 75:650-655.

FT_EC7_reason: Phone/SM are NOT only dependent variables; they are independent variables (predictors of sleep). The analysis is phone/SM → sleep outcome (non-SM outcome), which means FT-EC7 does not apply. This is a standard phone/SM exposure analysis.
notes: Prospective design strengthens analysis. Both device presence (TV/electronic in bedroom) and specific activities (texting, social media, music, video games) measured separately with individual coefficients. All bedtime screen modalities prospectively associated with adverse sleep outcomes.
